model.label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Wisconsin holds a commanding ~15-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage over High Point (KenPom #22, AdjEM +23.4 vs. KenPom #92, AdjEM +8.4), with a top-11 nationally ranked offense that is perfectly positioned to exploit the Panthers' defense ranked 148th by Torvik — the most glaring weakness in High Point's otherwise impressive 30-4 profile. The Badgers received a critical health boost with both Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist) returning to practice ahead of the tournament, putting Wisconsin near full strength at the right time. The betting market's 11.5-point spread reflects the consistent edge across all metrics, and while High Point's elite offense (3rd nationally in scoring margin at +19.7) and the Rob Martin/Cam Fletcher scoring duo give them a credible upset path, their Big South schedule (SOS ranked 320th nationally) leaves them wholly unprepared for Wisconsin's Big Ten-caliber physicality and defensive discipline. The 5-vs-12 historical upset rate warrants respect, but High Point's defensive deficiencies make this one of the less credible 12-seed upset scenarios in the bracket.

reasoningStep.title: Wisconsin vs. High Point — Round of 64 West Region Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Wisconsin is a clear favorite backed by a ~15-point efficiency gap, a top-11 offense nationally, and a returning-to-health roster. High Point's elite offense is a genuine threat but their weak defense (148th by Torvik) and soft Big South schedule undercut their upset credentials against a Big Ten-tested Wisconsin squad.
evidence: Wisconsin: KenPom #22, AdjEM +23.4, AdjO 125.4 (11th nationally), AdjD 102.0; Torvik #21, AdjOE 127.2 (8th), AdjDE 101.6 (48th). High Point: KenPom #92, AdjEM +8.4, AdjO 117.1 (66th), AdjD 108.7 (148th by Torvik). Efficiency gap of ~15 points favors Wisconsin in every dimension.

Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist) both returned to practice Monday and took a 'big jump' per coach Greg Gard per Badger Notes, putting Wisconsin near full strength heading into the tournament opener.
Wisconsin opened as an 11.5-point favorite over High Point with a total of 167.5, per Action Network — market consensus strongly and consistently favors the Badgers.

High Point: 30-4 record, Big South regular season and tournament champion, ORtg 124.5 (3rd nationally per Sports Reference), but SOS ranked 320th nationally — a severe schedule quality gap versus Wisconsin's Big Ten slate.

Yahoo Sports West Regional breakdown flagged Wisconsin vs. High Point as the best Round 1 matchup in the West, noting High Point's +19.7 scoring margin as the primary upset vehicle but Wisconsin's defensive profile and Big Ten experience as decisive advantages.

result.text: Wisconsin nearing full strength as Nolan Winter, Jack Janicki return to practice Badgernotes Subscribe Sign in Basketball Wisconsin nearing full strength as Nolan Winter, Jack Janicki return to practice Greg Gard said Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki took a "big jump" in practice as Wisconsin prepares to face High Point in the NCAA Tournament. Dillon Graff Mar 16, 2026 3 Share Wisconsin Badgers players Nolan Winter, John Blackwell, and Jack Janicki stand next to each other on the court. Photo credit: UW Athletics The University of Wisconsin men’s basketball team may be getting closer to full strength at the right time. Subscribe After spending the last few weeks navigating the absence of two key rotational pieces, the 5th-seeded Badgers received one of their most encouraging updates yet from head coach Greg Gard after practice ahead of Wisconsin’s NCAA Tournament opener against 12-seed High Point. Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist), both sidelined with injuries, took a big step forward Monday afternoon. The duo returned to practice after missing time during a stretch in which the Badgers went 6–2 without Janicki and 3–1 in games where both he and Winter were unavailable. “Both of them were in full-go mode, other than contact stuff, major contact stuff. So it’s the most any of them have done,” Gard said . “They both ran full court in some skeleton stuff we did, both were in the drills. It was a big jump today, and everyone felt pretty good coming out of it, so the plan is to take another step tomorrow.” @badgermbb Wisconsin Basketball on Instagram: "🔜" In other words, Wisconsin may soon have its full rotation of preferred options available for the big dance. That possibility matters for a team built around a balanced rotation and an inside-out frontcourt presence, with Winter serving as one of the core pieces that anchors much of what the Badgers do. Getting Janicki back would also provide another versatile option off the bench — a player whose defensive activity and ability to guard multiple spots have quietly added value throughout the season. Winter, a 7-foot forward, has been one of the most reliable players on the roster this season. He enters the NCAA Tournament averaging 13.3 points, a team-leading 8.6 rebounds per game, while shooting an efficient 56.9% from the field. His production on the glass has been especially important. Winter has recorded 12 double-doubles this season, the second-most in the Big Ten conference, and has reached double-digit rebounds 14 times. Durability had also become part of the Lakeville North product’s identity before the recent absence. Winter had started 68 straight games and appeared in 104 consecutive contests for Wisconsin before an ankle injury suffered against Maryland on March 4 brought that streak to an end. Janicki’s situation has been a little different, but it still created a noticeable gap in the rotation as one of Wisconsin’s trusted reserves. The 6-foot-5 guard suffere

result.title: No. 12 High Point to Face No. 5 Wisconsin in NCAA Tournament
result.snippet: Since becoming Division I in 1999, HPU has been to the NCAA Tournament two times (2024-25 & 2025-26). HPU's 12 seed is the highest seeding in program history. A season ago, the Panthers faced No. 4 seed Purdue in the first round as a No. 13 seed . HPU won its second consecutive Big South Championship Title (2024-25 & 2025-26).

result.text: Wisconsin nearing full strength as Nolan Winter, Jack Janicki return to practice Badgernotes Subscribe Sign in Basketball Wisconsin nearing full strength as Nolan Winter, Jack Janicki return to practice Greg Gard said Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki took a "big jump" in practice as Wisconsin prepares to face High Point in the NCAA Tournament. Dillon Graff Mar 16, 2026 3 Share Wisconsin Badgers players Nolan Winter, John Blackwell, and Jack Janicki stand next to each other on the court. Photo credit: UW Athletics The University of Wisconsin men’s basketball team may be getting closer to full strength at the right time. Subscribe After spending the last few weeks navigating the absence of two key rotational pieces, the 5th-seeded Badgers received one of their most encouraging updates yet from head coach Greg Gard after practice ahead of Wisconsin’s NCAA Tournament opener against 12-seed High Point. Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist), both sidelined with injuries, took a big step forward Monday afternoon. The duo returned to practice after missing time during a stretch in which the Badgers went 6–2 without Janicki and 3–1 in games where both he and Winter were unavailable. “Both of them were in full-go mode, other than contact stuff, major contact stuff. So it’s the most any of them have done,” Gard said . “They both ran full court in some skeleton stuff we did, both were in the drills. It was a big jump today, and everyone felt pretty good coming out of it, so the plan is to take another step tomorrow.” @badgermbb Wisconsin Basketball on Instagram: "🔜" In other words, Wisconsin may soon have its full rotation of preferred options available for the big dance. That possibility matters for a team built around a balanced rotation and an inside-out frontcourt presence, with Winter serving as one of the core pieces that anchors much of what the Badgers do. Getting Janicki back would also provide another versatile option off the bench — a player whose defensive activity and ability to guard multiple spots have quietly added value throughout the season. Winter, a 7-foot forward, has been one of the most reliable players on the roster this season. He enters the NCAA Tournament averaging 13.3 points, a team-leading 8.6 rebounds per game, while shooting an efficient 56.9% from the field. His production on the glass has been especially important. Winter has recorded 12 double-doubles this season, the second-most in the Big Ten conference, and has reached double-digit rebounds 14 times. Durability had also become part of the Lakeville North product’s identity before the recent absence. Winter had started 68 straight games and appeared in 104 consecutive contests for Wisconsin before an ankle injury suffered against Maryland on March 4 brought that streak to an end. Janicki’s situation has been a little different, but it still created a noticeable gap in the rotation as one of Wisconsin’s trusted reserves. The 6-foot-5 guard suffere

The Badgers received a critical health boost with both Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist) returning to practice ahead of the tournament, putting Wisconsin near full strength at the right time. The betting market's 11.5-point spread reflects the consistent edge across all metrics, and while High Point's elite offense (3rd nationally in scoring margin at +19.7) and the Rob Martin/Cam Fletcher scoring duo give them a credible upset path, their Big South schedule (SOS ranked 320th nationally) leaves them wholly unprepared for Wisconsin's Big Ten-caliber physicality and defensive discipline.
